tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • VMOVUPD: 6 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 4 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
12 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
3 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
3 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).
9 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 167 FP arithmetical operations:
  • 102: addition or subtraction (65 inside FMA instructions)
  • 65: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 1304 bytes (163 double precision FP elements). The binary loop is storing 572 bytes (71 double precision FP elements).

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 32.67 cycles. At this rate:
  • 41% of peak load performance is reached (39.92 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 27% of peak store performance is reached (17.51 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.67 to 28.67 cycles (1.14x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.67 to 25.33 cycles (1.29x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
55% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
  • 53% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 50% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 28% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 78% of SSE/AVX fused multiply-add instructions are used in vector version.
  • 55% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 49% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.67 to 22.93 cycles (1.42x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
  • VMOVUPD: 6 occurrences<>
hint.title: Complex instructions

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:
  1. align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2. inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
<>
hint.title: Vector unaligned load/store instructions
hint.txt: Detected 4 optimal vector unaligned load/store instructions.

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt:
12 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
3 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (two at a time).
3 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).
9 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 167 FP arithmetical operations:
  • 102: addition or subtraction (65 inside FMA instructions)
  • 65: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 1304 bytes (163 double precision FP elements). The binary loop is storing 572 bytes (71 double precision FP elements).

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 32.67 cycles. At this rate:
  • 41% of peak load performance is reached (39.92 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 27% of peak store performance is reached (17.51 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.67 to 28.67 cycles (1.14x speedup).

header: 21% of peak computational performance is used (5.11 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.67 to 25.33 cycles (1.29x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: C storage order is row-major: for(i) for(j) a[j][i] = b[j][i]; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) for(j) a[i][j] = b[i][j]; (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): for(i) a[i].x = b[i].x; (slow, non stride 1) => for(i) a.x[i] = b.x[i]; (fast, stride 1)

Your loop is partially vectorized.
Only 49% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 32.67 to 22.93 cycles (1.42x speedup).
